title: Georgia
text:

Currently Eligible forParole but in Prison
Eligible for Parole inthe next 1-5 years










Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationOffensesReleases from PrisonDisparities








How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

While the grant of parole is discretionary, consideration for parole is often not; most inmates are entitled to automatic consideration for parole. In general, this occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence. However, at minimum, a person serving a misdemeanor sentence must serve 6 months and a person serving a felony sentence must serve 9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.


People who were Eligible for Parole

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison, accounting for 59% (10,941 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.







In 2020, there were 847 people who were incarcerated for a parole revocation and were eligible for parole but not yet released from prison.





















Prison Population

In 2020, there were 18,701 people who were eligible for parole but not released from prison, constituting 40% of the parole-eligible prison population.























Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 5,377 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 29% of the parole-eligible prison population.

title: National Trends
text:
With the support of Arnold Ventures, the Council of State Governments (CSG) Justice Center analyzed data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) and Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice to gain deeper insights into the relationship between parole practices and prison populations. The study specifically focused on understanding the dynamics of parole eligibility and lengths of stay, with a specific emphasis on examining racial, ethnic, and gender disparities.

Parole Eligibility Data by State


The table provides a comprehensive overview of state-by-state data on the number of people in prison and their parole eligibility status, including the percentage of individuals currently eligible for parole, those who will be eligible in the next 1-5 years, and the proportion for which parole eligibility data is missing or low, due to the abolition of parole or lack of data in certain states.

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

Arkansas has a bifurcated prison release eligibility process. Some offenders are automatically “transfer eligible” to parole and are released upon first eligibility unless specific (negative) criteria are met. Other offenders are “discretionary transfer eligible” and can be released only at the discretion of the Board through the transfer process or through standard parole release.

People who were Eligible for Parole

In 2020, Hispanic, any race people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison, accounting for 36% (3,207 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.







In 2020, there were 1,407 people who were incarcerated for a parole revocation and were eligible for parole but not yet released from prison.





















Prison Population

In 2020, there were 8,899 people who were eligible for parole but not released from prison, constituting 9% of the parole-eligible prison population.























Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 1,253 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 14% of the parole-eligible prison population.
